segments.text: Hi everyone, I'm Stefan from the Freewings flight school and today I'd like to tell you a bit about aerodynamics.
Aerodynamics in general, but mainly paraglider-specific.
The question that surely occupies all of us is why a paraglider flies at all.
It hasn't let go of me since the first moment I saw a paraglider as a child.
I'm always fascinated by why a paraglider made of fabric and a few lines, pulled out of a backpack, can lift us into the air and let us fly with it.
If you get to the bottom of it, you quickly find that a paraglider works exactly the same way as any aeroplane, like any bird too.
We have a wing that generates lift, which produces a force that keeps us in the air.
I sometimes compare it with a car: you can drive any car, but whether you really understand how the clutch and engine and everything works isn't usually the decisive thing.
With a paraglider too, you can be a super top pilot and not really know what's going on above you.
But I'm convinced that, when paragliding, it's relatively important

compared to car driving — that you also understand the technical background a bit, because it contributes to safety, and also, if you want to get the full performance out of the wing later when things get a bit more ambitious, having background knowledge about aerodynamics lets you get more out of your wing.
It all started a very, very long time ago.
He started experimenting in 1891 and built the first, you can almost say, hang gliders, and even carried out the first successful flights.
He started early on observing birds and realised that not all birds need to flap their wings to stay in the air.
He mainly looked at the larger birds and noticed that they can glide quite well without doing anything.
And then he started taking storks apart, dissecting dead animals, and noticed that the wings of these animals all have a certain airfoil shape.
He then began transferring that wing profile to his own wings and discovered that you can generate lift that carries you into the air after just a few running steps.
In general, there are several attempts to explain, or reasons, why a wing flies.
Basically there are three approaches, or three principles, why a wing generates lift and keeps us in the air.
The first approach is Bernoulli's theory, and that has to do with fluid dynamics.
You can see here we have an airfoil section that, like most airfoils, is asymmetric.
That means there's more curvature on the top than on the bottom.
This causes the air on top to have to circulate faster, to flow faster, and that creates lower pressure.
On the underside of the wing, it's exactly the opposite.
There the airflow is slower and higher pressure builds up.
You can see the low-pressure and high-pressure regions are split into countless little forces.
If you bundle them all together and combine all the forces at one point, one resultant force emerges.
That is the lift that allows us to fly, the force that acts upward.
The split between them is a little tip for the theory exam.
The split is two-thirds low pressure on the upper surface and one-third high pressure on the lower surface.
You have to watch out not to mix things up here.
At some point the flow stops following the profile and starts to separate back here.
We call that the separation point — back here it creates small turbulence and somewhat chaotic air.
We'll come back to that in detail later.
fluid dynamics, low pressure, high pressure — the first approach to why the wing flies.
Then the second is the recoil principle.
That's Newton's action equals reaction — he found out that on every body where some force acts, an opposing force is generated.
Maybe you've tried it: when you stick your hand out of a moving car —
perhaps not as the driver, perhaps as a passenger paying close attention to what exactly happens —
if you hold your hand straight out,
not much happens, you don't feel much drag, because the hand lies flat in the wind.
But if you tilt your hand a little, in that direction, there's a force that pulls upward.
And if you tilt it the other way, it pushes your hand downward instead.
And the wing actually uses this recoil principle too,
because the wing also has a certain angle to the oncoming air.
We call that the angle of attack — we'll come back to it later.
And this too generates a force that points upward and keeps us in the air.
The third approach for why a wing generates lift is the so-called circulation flow.
At the sharp trailing edge of the wing a starting vortex develops and that flow is deflected downward.
And this in turn gives a moment or force upward,
which also contributes to this dynamic lift.
So those are the three approaches that keep the wing in the air.
Now we come to a few technical terms regarding airfoils.
We're still looking at the wing in cross-section for the moment.
Later we'll look at the whole thing as a paraglider, the whole wing across the span,
and the lines and the pilot hanging underneath, and what influence that has.
For the moment we're just looking at the cross-section.
And on paragliders the profile is relatively thick.
You can see here, the profile thickness is the blue bar.
It's relatively thick compared to, for example, a fast aircraft —
a fighter jet, where the profile would be very flat, less asymmetric and rather thinner.
So slow aircraft, which already produce a lot of lift at a low launch speed,
without having to generate huge speed first,
are designed to be rather thick and asymmetric.
Chord length is essentially the distance from the front-most point to the rear-most point.
On the paraglider, leading edge and trailing edge — terms you know,
which you'll keep using again and again in flight practice.
Chord line is more of a theoretical term.
It's the straight line connecting the front-most and rear-most points.
Not to be confused with the chord length.
And the chord line is really the line connecting those two points.
Now we come to the forces and vectors.
I mentioned briefly that there are multiple force vectors on a wing because of this lift.
And these can all be summed up into one main vector, this lift vector.
that this is the total aerodynamic force, composed of lift and drag.
First, theoretically, how it looks with the vectors.
This is a classic theory exam question.
